request: In 220 to 320 words, frame this computational result for an operational audience. Formulation B is equivalent to Formulation A and solved the supplied benchmark instances 42% faster. A feasible route must be committed by 15 minutes; the optimality certificate arrives later. Explain the valid contribution, what the structural numbers mean, and what evidence would be needed for a profit or service claim. Do not imply that equivalence changes the true optimum or that post-deadline proof improves the executed route.
output_a:
Formulation B is a computational improvement, not a different operational routing model. Because it is equivalent to Formulation A, both formulations permit the same feasible routes and share the same true optimum for a given instance. Its valid contribution is solver efficiency: on the supplied benchmark instances, B solved 42% faster. That number describes computational performance under the tested instances, solver configuration, hardware, and stopping conditions; it does not mean routes are 42% cheaper, more profitable, or more reliable.

The relevant operational constraint is the 15-minute commitment deadline. B has operational value if it produces a feasible route by that deadline when A cannot, or if it produces a demonstrably better feasible incumbent by minute 15. Faster solution can also matter if it permits more scenario evaluations or reoptimization cycles before dispatch. The optimality certificate arriving after 15 minutes establishes that the already-executed route was optimal under the model, but it cannot improve that route or its realized outcome after commitment.

**Evidence needed:** A profit or service claim is **not_supplied** by the benchmark result alone. It would require a pre-deadline comparison of the feasible routes available from A and B at minute 15; an explicit, validated mapping from the routing objective to profit, cost, lateness, or service measures; and tests on representative operational instances and conditions. Field pilots or historical back-testing should then measure realized outcomes, including implementation effects such as execution delays, route adherence, and disruptions.

**Boundary:** The 42% result is **conditionally_supported** only for the supplied benchmark environment. It should not be generalized to other instance families, solvers, hardware, time limits, or operating settings without corresponding evidence.
output_b:
For the benchmark-defined routing decision, Formulation B preserves the same feasible routes and the same true optimal objective value as Formulation A. Its valid contribution is computational: it solved the supplied benchmark instances 42% faster. This can matter operationally because a dispatcher must commit a feasible route within 15 minutes. If B reliably returns an acceptable feasible route before that cutoff when A does not, it expands the team’s ability to make a timely, model-consistent decision.

The structural numbers have distinct meanings. “Equivalent” means B does not create better routes or change the underlying optimum; it is a different representation of the same decision problem. “42% faster” is a benchmark runtime result, not a 42% improvement in route quality, cost, profit, or service. The 15-minute threshold is the decision window: only solution information available before it can affect the executed route. An optimality certificate received afterward can verify that the committed route was optimal under the model, but it cannot improve a route already put into operation.

The contribution is therefore **supported** as a formulation-level speed improvement on the supplied instances. Its operational value is **conditionally_supported**: it depends on whether the tested speed advantage persists under the actual solver, hardware, time limit, instance mix, and dispatch workflow, and whether it changes the pre-deadline incumbent available to the dispatcher.

A profit or service claim is **not_supplied**. To support one, evidence must link pre-deadline routing choices to observed outcomes—for example, route-level operating costs and margins for profit, or on-time arrival, delay, cancellation, and customer-wait measures for service—against a relevant baseline, ideally through a controlled pilot or credible before/after design. The principal boundary is that benchmark runtime alone does not establish performance beyond the tested computational conditions.
